text.content: A learner oriented learning recommendation approach based on mixed concept mapping and immune algorithm

title.plain_text: A learner oriented learning recommendation approach based on mixed concept mapping and immune algorithm

tables.heading: Future work
rows: Sequence of Learning Objectives meant to achieve the learner’s goals

Learners are initially modelled by survey answers.

Learning Objectives are defined by basic variables and Expert Experience and Previous Feedbacks

Done using an Immune Algorithm:

Optimization done via a fitness function at the end of each recommendation.

Relationship between Learners and LOs is done via Mixed Concept Mapping - uncovered via Association Rule Mining. 

Mixed concept map is updated by learner interactions with the system.
Top-1 Learning Objective Learner should follow

Fitness function: 
0,06 (IA)

Number of Recommended LOs: 
144 LOs (IA)

Time on Recommended LOs: 137 (IA)

Evolution Time (s): 4.7 (IA)

Satisfaction Survey:
IA achieved highest gobal satisfaction score, especially when it comes to Flow experience Evaluation and Pertinence.

Optimization of the recommender algorithm is done via the Immune Algorithm.

Comparison is done versus groups of students who received recommendations done by other algorithms: GA, Ant Colony Optimization (ACO);
PSO, Traditional Teaching Metods.

Model trained via IA outperforms all other across all metrics tested: students achieve better performance by learning from less materials and spending less time on them.

Requires learners to fill questionnaire for user intialization.

Small sample sie (requries validation on other datasets).

blocks.text: Introduction

text.content: Introduction

rich_text.plain_text: Introduction

blocks.text: In an unstructured scenario of learning such as MOOCs, it often becomes challenging to enhance the learning experience which results in high dropout and low acquisition of knowledge. Personalized learning can assist in the creation of proper learning objectives that are suited to the learners.

text.content: In an unstructured scenario of learning such as MOOCs, it often becomes challenging to enhance the learning experience which results in high dropout and low acquisition of knowledge. Personalized learning can assist in the creation of proper learning objectives that are suited to the learners.

blocks.text: The authors focus heavily on content-based filtering and discuss that these systems can be effective in the generation of learning paths that match the learner’s goals. However, this involves creeating an adequate Constraint Satisfaction Problem that matches competency, Learning objectives and other constraints - a very complex task.

blocks.text: Previous work has focused on identifying learner needs and making recommendations that would address those difficulties - sometimes making adaptations along the way. However, there are also other factors that influence learning that have not been accounted for.

text.content: Previous work has focused on identifying learner needs and making recommendations that would address those difficulties - sometimes making adaptations along the way. However, there are also other factors that influence learning that have not been accounted for.

blocks.text: In this work, the authors start with the application of concept mapping to get a sense of the learner and the Learning Objectives. The novelty comes from the fact that it is a mixed concept mapping that take the learner’s attributes and learning goals to combine them into a LO concept map. Genetic Algorithms have also been used for this task, but the authors argue that it is vulnerable to parameter setting.

blocks.text: The authors put a clear emphasis on matching learning objectives with the learning style. The reason for this is straightforward: if a learner’s learning goals are not mappable to a learning objective then there is more incentive for the learner to not learn - and possibly fail the course.

blocks.text: Following the rules mined often leads to a better learning experience and, consequently, a better learning outcome. Straying away from it is argued to lead to tiredness or depression. This form of association rule mining allows the identification of a pathway of a student based on his/her attributes. However, it may be considered very inneficient computationally.

blocks.text: To address the inefficiency, the authors also created a dynamic k-means approach that takes as input the learner attributes as centroids to cluster the learning resources. So… you take information about the students and use them as centroids - Then, try to find which learning resources are “more similar” to the current centroids (this part is somewhat unclear to me, may need to revise it if important).

blocks.text: IA seems to generate pretty satisfactory results from the author's POV.

blocks.text: Students who had recommendations done from this method had more free time, checked less resources and were still among the top performing members at the end of the semester. Overall, IA also was the option that was more positively received by the student population.

blocks.text:
The experimental approach of using the Mixed Concept Mapping and the Vaccine innoculation also showed promising results as the algorithm seemed to not only converge fast, but to also converge closer to optimal solutions.

In general, the study is a good showcase about the buzz around personalized learning. When leaving students unattended on what to study or how to study it they tend to follow what can be considered a lot of inneficient and/or redundant paths which may lead to a low feeling of progress on a larger amount of time dedicated and, consequently, higher degrees of frustration and demotivation.

blocks.text: Future work

text.content: Future work

rich_text.plain_text: Future work

blocks.text:
The authors propose a different way of representing students that considers their knowledge but also their motivations and preferences. The relationship between learners and resources was uncovered using a mixed concept mapping that, among other things, uses Association Rules.

Making, for every student, a recommendation based on the association rules uncovered is a computationally impossible task, which brings us to the use of Optimization Algorithms to make those recommendations. The authors design a special Immune Algorithm and compare how well it performs against other optimization algorithms such as PSO and GA.

text.content:
The authors propose a different way of representing students that considers their knowledge but also their motivations and preferences. The relationship between learners and resources was uncovered using a mixed concept mapping that, among other things, uses Association Rules.

Making, for every student, a recommendation based on the association rules uncovered is a computationally impossible task, which brings us to the use of Optimization Algorithms to make those recommendations. The authors design a special Immune Algorithm and compare how well it performs against other optimization algorithms such as PSO and GA.

The authors propose a different way of representing students that considers their knowledge but also their motivations and preferences. The relationship between learners and resources was uncovered using a mixed concept mapping that, among other things, uses Association Rules.

Making, for every student, a recommendation based on the association rules uncovered is a computationally impossible task, which brings us to the use of Optimization Algorithms to make those recommendations. The authors design a special Immune Algorithm and compare how well it performs against other optimization algorithms such as PSO and GA.

blocks.text: Future work will consider improvements to the performance of the algorithm and the way student knowledge is represented.
